Retained for backwards compatibility with  earlier versions of VCF and older VCF indexing software which rely on this field being present.

[VCF v4.5 — Fixed fields] This is a computed field that, when present, must be set to the maximum end reference position (1-based) of:
the position of the final base of the REF allele,
the end position corresponding to the SVLEN of a symbolic SV allele,
and the end positions calculated from FORMAT LEN for the <*> symbolic allele.

[VCF v4.5 — Fixed fields] The computed value of this field is used to compute BCF's rlen field (see <ref>) and is important when indexing VCF/BCF files to enable random access and querying by position.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] If genotype information is present, then the same types of data must be present for all samples.
First a FORMAT field is given specifying the data types and order (colon-separated FORMAT keys matching the regular expression , duplicate keys are not allowed).
This is followed by one data block per sample, with the colon-separated data corresponding to the types specified in the format.
The first key must always be the genotype (GT) if it is present.
If any local-allele field is present, LAA must also be present and precede all fields other than GT.
There are no required keys.
Additional Genotype keys can be defined in the meta-information, however, software support for them is not guaranteed.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] If any of the fields is missing, it is replaced with the MISSING value.
For example if the FORMAT is GT:GQ:DP:HQ then 0|0:.:23:23,34 indicates that GQ is missing.
If a field contains a list of missing values, it can be represented either as a single MISSING value (`.') or as a list of missing values (e.g. `.,.,.' if the field was Number=3).
Trailing fields can be dropped, with the exception of the GT field, which should always be present if specified in the FORMAT field.
If a field and it's local-allele equivalent are both defined they must encode identical information or one must ignored by containing the MISSING value or omitted.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] As with the INFO field, there are several common, reserved keywords that are standards across the community.
See their detailed definitions below, as well as Table <ref> for their reference Number, Type and Description.
See also Section <ref> for a list of genotype keys reserved for structural variants.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * AD, ADF, ADR (Integer): Per-sample read depths for each allele; total (AD), on the forward (ADF) and the reverse (ADR) strand.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * DP (Integer): Read depth at this position for this sample.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * EC (Integer): Comma separated list of expected alternate allele counts for each alternate allele in the same order as listed in the ALT field.
  Typically used in association analyses.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * LEN (Integer): length of the <*> reference block for this sample.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * FT (String): Sample genotype filter indicating if this genotype was “called” (similar in concept to the FILTER field).
  Again, use PASS to indicate that all filters have been passed, a semicolon-separated list of codes for filters that fail, or `.' to indicate that filters have not been applied.
  These values should be described in the meta-information in the same way as FILTERs.
  No whitespace or semicolons permitted.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * GQ (Integer): Conditional genotype quality, encoded as a phred quality -10log_10 p(genotype call is wrong, conditioned on the site's being variant).
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * GP (Float): Genotype posterior probabilities in the range 0 to 1 using the same ordering as the GL field; one use can be to store imputed genotype probabilities.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * GT (String): Genotype, encoded as allele value preceded by either of / or | depending on whether that allele is considered phased. 
    The first phasing indicator may be omitted and is implicitly defined as / if any phasing indicators are / and | otherwise.
    The allele values are 0 for the reference allele (what is in the REF field), 1 for the first allele listed in ALT, 2 for the second allele list in ALT and so on.
    For diploid calls examples could be 0/1, 1|0, /0/1, or 1/2, etc.
    Haploid calls, e.g. on Y, male non-pseudoautosomal X, or mitochondria, should be indicated by having only one allele value.
    A triploid call might look like 0/0/1, and a partially phased triploid call could be |0/1/2 to indicate that the first allele is phased with another variant in the VCF.
    If a call cannot be made for a sample at a given locus, `.' must be specified for each missing allele in the GT field (for example `./.' for a diploid genotype and `.' for haploid genotype).
    The meanings of the phasing indicators are as follows (see the PS and PSL fields below for more details on incorporating phasing information into the genotypes):
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * / : allele is unphased
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * | : allele is phased (according to the phase-set indicated in PS or PSL)

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] For symbolic structural variant alleles, GT=0 indicates the absence of any of the ALT symbolic structural variants defined in the record.
    Implementer should note that merging a VCF record containing only symbolic structural variant ALT alleles with a record containing other alleles will result a change of the meaning of the GT=0 haplotypes from the record containing only symbolic SVs.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * GL (Float): Genotype likelihoods comprised of comma separated floating point log_10-scaled likelihoods for all possible genotypes given the set of alleles defined in the REF and ALT fields.
  In presence of the GT field the same ploidy is expected; without GT field, diploidy is assumed.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Genotype Ordering. 
  In general case of ploidy P and N alternate alleles (0 is the REF and 1… N the alternate alleles), the ordering of genotypes for the likelihoods can be expressed by the following pseudocode with as many nested loops as ploidy:
  [Note that we use inclusive  loop boundaries.]

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] for a_P = 0… N
    for a_P-1 = 0… a_P
        …
        for a_1 = 0… a_2
            println a_1 a_2  …  a_P
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Alternatively, the same can be achieved recursively with the following pseudocode:

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Ordering(P, N, suffix=""):
        for a in 0… N
            if (P == 1) println str(a) + suffix
            if (P > 1) Ordering(P-1, a, str(a) + suffix)
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Conversely, the index of the value corresponding to the genotype k_1≤ k_2≤…≤ k_P is
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Index(k_1/k_2/…/k_P) = ∑_m=1^Pk_m + m - 1  m
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * for P=2 and N=1, the ordering is 00,01,11
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * for P=2 and N=2, the ordering is 00,01,11,02,12,22
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * for P=3 and N=2, the ordering is 000, 001, 011, 111, 002, 012, 112, 022, 122, 222
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * for P=1, the index of the genotype a is a
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * for P=2, the index of the genotype “a/b”, where a≤ b, is b (b+1)/2 + a

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * for P=2 and arbitrary N, the ordering can be easily derived from a triangular matrix
            5em
               b∖ a     0     1     2     3
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] 3       6     7     8     9
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * HQ (Integer): Haplotype qualities, two comma separated phred qualities.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * LAA is a list of n distinct integers, giving the 1-based indices of the ALT alleles that are observed in the sample.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] In callsets with many samples, sites may grow to include numerous alternate alleles at the same POS.
  Usually, few of these alleles are actually observed in any one sample, but each genotype must supply fields like PL and AD for all of the alleles—a very inefficient representation as PL's size is quadratic in the allele count.
  Similarly, in rare sites, which can be the bulk of the sites, the vast majority of the samples are reference.
  To prevent this growth in VCF size, one can choose to specify the genotype, allele depth and the genotype likelihood against a subset of “Local Alleles”.
  LAA is the 1-based index into ALT, defining the alleles that are actually in-play for that sample and the order in which they are interpreted.
  LAA is required when interpreting local-allele fields and must be present if any local-allele fields are neither omitted nor MISSING.
  Since BCF encodes zero length vectors as MISSING, a LAA containing the MISSING value should be treated as the empty vector (i.e. a REF-only site) if any local-allele fields are neither omitted nor MISSING.
  All specifications-defined A, R and G FORMAT fields have a local-allele equivalent that should be interpreted in the same manner as it's matching field except for the ALT alleles considered present and the order in which they are interpreted.
  For example, if REF is G, ALT is A,C,T, and a genotype only has information about G, C, and , one can have LAA=[2,4] and thus LPL will be interpreted as pertaining to the alleles [G, C, ] and not contain likelihood values for genotypes that involve A or T.
  GQ is still the genotype quality, even when the genotype is given against the local alleles.
  In the following example, the records with the same POS encode the same information (some columns removed for clarity):

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] l]llllll
  	POS    REF     ALT   FORMAT   sample
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] 2   A   C,G,T,*    GT:LAA:LAD:LPL    0/3:3:15,25:40,0,80
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] 3   C   G,T,*    GT:LAA:LAD:LPL    0/0:3:30,1:0,30,80
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] 4   G   A,T,*    GT:LAA:LAD:LPL    0/0::30:0

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Due to BCF encoding empty vectors as missing, implementation-defined Number=LA local-allele fields should not be used if distinguishing between zero-length data and missing data is required at REF-only sites.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] It is recommended that VCF libraries provide an API in which local allele encoding can be abstracted away from the API consumer and values accessed through their corresponding non-local key.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * LPL: is a list of n Ploidy integers giving phred-scaled genotype likelihoods (rounded to the closest integer; as per PL) for all possible genotypes given the set of alleles defined in the LAA local alleles.
  The precise ordering is defined in the GL paragraph.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * M[0-9]+[ACGTUN] (Float): Fraction of DNA or RNA bases modified with the given ChEBI ID.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] All FORMAT keys matching the given regular expression are considered reserved keys, even for ChEBI IDs that do not correspond to valid base modifications.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] The alias keys M5mC, M5hmC, M5fC, M5caC, M5hmU, M5fU, M5caU, M6mA, M8oxoG, and MxaoN should be used instead of their corresponding ChEBI keys.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Values must be between 0 and 1 and indicate how prevalent the modified base is in the sample.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] When base modification information is present in the FORMAT field of a reference block record, the base modification information apply to all applicable bases covered by that reference block.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * DPM[0-9]+[ACGTUN] (Integer): Total read depth for reads able to detect the base modification with the given ChEBI ID.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] All FORMAT keys matching the given regular expression are considered reserved keys, even for ChEBI IDs that do not correspond to valid base modifications.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] The alias keys DPM5mC, DPM5hmC, DPM5fC, DPM5caC, DPM5hmU, DPM5fU, DPM5caU, DPM6mA, DPM8oxoG, and DPMxaoN should be used instead of their corresponding ChEBI keys.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * ADM[0-9]+[ACGTUN] (Integer): Read depth for reads with the base modification with the given ChEBI ID.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] All FORMAT keys matching the given regular expression are considered reserved keys, even for ChEBI IDs that do not correspond to valid base modifications.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] The alias keys ADM5mC, ADM5hmC, ADM5fC, ADM5caC, ADM5hmU, ADM5fU, ADM5caU, ADM6mA, ADM8oxoG, and ADMxaoN should be used instead of their corresponding ChEBI keys.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Note that ADFM[0-9]+[ACGTUN] and ADRM[0-9]+[ACGTUN] are not reserved fields as Type=M fields are intrinsically stranded and unstranded information should be encoded using the MISSING value.
  Unstranded CpG methylation counts should be placed in the C position with value for the subsequent G base MISSING.
  Stranded CpG methylation counts should be placed in both values with the C position effectively encoding ADF, and the G effectively encoding ADR due to the strand the C in the CpG occurs on.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] The follow example contains unphased, unstranded CpG methylation information for the CpG at chr:10-11 and phased, stranded CpG methylation information for the CpG at chr:20-21.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] #CHROM     POS     REF     ALT     FORMAT     SAMPLE
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr     10     C     .             GT:M5mC:DPM5mC:ADM5mC           0/0:0.5:2:1
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr     11     G     .             GT:M5mC:DPM5mC:ADM5mC           0/0:.:.:.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr     20     C     .             GT:PS:M5mC:DPM5mC:ADM5mC           0|0:20:0.75,.:4,.:3,.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr     21     G     A             GT:PS:M5mC:DPM5mC:ADM5mC           0|1:20:0.33:3:1
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Note that in the above example, the second record could be omitted entirely without any change in meaning.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * MQ (Integer): RMS mapping quality, similar to the version in the INFO field.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PL (Integer): The phred-scaled genotype likelihoods rounded to the closest integer, and otherwise defined in the same way as the GL field.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PP (Integer): The phred-scaled genotype posterior probabilities rounded to the closest integer, and otherwise defined in the same way as the GP field.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PQ (Integer): Phasing quality, the phred-scaled probability that alleles are ordered incorrectly in a heterozygote (against all other members in the phase set).
  We note that we have not yet included the specific measure for precisely defining “phasing quality”; our intention for now is simply to reserve the PQ tag for future use as a measure of phasing quality.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PS (non-negative 32-bit Integer): Phase set, defined as a set of phased genotypes to which this genotype belongs.
  Phased genotypes for an individual that are on the same chromosome and have the same PS value are in the same phased set.
  A phase set specifies multi-marker haplotypes for the phased genotypes in the set.
  All phased genotypes that do not contain a PS subfield are assumed to belong to the same phased set.
  If the genotype in the GT field is unphased, the corresponding PS field is ignored.
  The recommended convention is to use the position of the first variant in the set as the PS identifier (although this is not required).

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PSL (List of Strings): The list of phase sets, one for each allele value specified in the GT. 
  Unphased alleles (without a | separator before them) must have the value '.' in their corresponding position in the list.
  Unlike PS (which is defined per CHROM), records with different CHROM but the same phase-set name are considered part of the same phase set.
  If an implementation cannot guarantee uniqueness of phase-set names across the VCF (for example, phasing a streaming VCF or each CHROM is processed independently in parallel), new phase-set names should be of the format CHROM*POS*ALLELE-NUMBER of the “first” allele which is included in this set, with ALLELE-NUMBER being the one-based index of the allele in the GT field, since multiple distinct phase-sets could start at the same position. [The `*' character is used as a separator since `:' is not reserved in the CHROM column.]
  A given sample-genotype must not have values for both PS and PSL.
  In addition, PS and PSL are not interoperable, in that a PS mentioned in one variant cannot be referenced in a PSL in another,  since when used in PS it isn't connected to any specific haplotype (i.e. first or second), but PSL is.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     SAMPLE1
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr19     5     .     T     G     .     PASS     DP=100    GT:PSL     |0/1:chr19*5*1,.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr20     10     .     A     T,G     .     PASS     DP=100    GT:PSL     |1/2|3:chr20*10*1,.,chr19*5*1
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr20     15     .     G     C     .     PASS     DP=100    GT:PSL     1|2:.,chr20*10*1

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PSO (List of integers): List of phase set ordinals.
  For each phase-set name, defines the order in which variants are encountered when traversing a derivative chromosome.
  The missing value '.' should be used when the corresponding PSO value is missing.
  For each phase-set name, PSO should be defined if any allele with that phase-set name on any record is symbolic structural variant or in breakpoint notation.
  Variants in breakpoint notation must have the same PSL and PSO on both records.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Without explicitly specifying the derivative chromosome traversal order, multiple derivative chromosome reconstructions are possible.
  Take for example this tandem duplication in a triploid organism with SNVs (ID/QUAL/FILTER columns removed for clarity):
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] #CHROM     POS     REF     ALT     INFO     FORMAT     SAMPLE1
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr1     10     T     <DUP>     SVCLAIM=DJ     GT:PSL:PSO     /0/0|1:.,.,chr1*10*3:.,.,3
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr1     20     A     G     .     GT:PSL:PSO     /0/0|0|1:.,.,chr1*10*1,chr1*10*3:.,.,4,1
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] chr1     30     G     T     .     GT:PSL:PSO     /0/0|0|1:.,.,chr1*10*1,chr1*10*3:.,.,2,5

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] Without defining PSO, it would be ambiguous as to which copy of the duplicated region the SNVs occur on.
  In this example, the presence of the PSO field clarifies that the SNVs are cis phased with the duplication, the first SNV occurs on the first copy of the duplicated region, and second SNV on the second copy.

[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] * PSQ (List of integers): The list of PQs, one for each phase set in PSL (encoded like PQ). 
  The missing value '.' should be used when the corresponding PSL value is missing, or when the phasing is of unknown quality.
[VCF v4.5 — Genotype fields] § UNDERSTANDING THE VCF FORMAT AND THE HAPLOTYPE REPRESENTATION

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p576
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] VCF records use a single general system for representing genetic variation data composed of:
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p577
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * Allele: representing single genetic haplotypes (A, T, ATC).
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p578
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * Genotype: an assignment of alleles for each chromosome of a single named sample at a particular locus.
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p579
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * VCF record: a record holding all segregating alleles at a locus (as well as genotypes, if appropriate, for multiple individuals containing alleles at that locus).
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p580
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] VCF records use a simple haplotype representation for REF and ALT alleles to describe variant haplotypes at a locus.
ALT haplotypes are constructed from the REF haplotype by taking the REF allele bases at the POS in the reference genotype and replacing them with the ALT bases.
In essence, the VCF record specifies a-REF-t and the alternative haplotypes are a-ALT-t for each alternative allele.
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p581
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] §.§ VCF tag naming conventions
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p582
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] Several tag names follow conventions which should be used for implementation-defined tag as well:
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p583
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * The `L' suffix means likelihood as log-likelihood in the sampling distribution, log_10(Data|Model).
    Likelihoods are represented as log_10 scale, thus they are negative numbers (e.g. GL, CNL).
    The likelihood can be also represented in some cases as phred-scale in a separate tag (e.g. PL).
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p584
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * The `P' suffix means probability as linear-scale probability in the posterior distribution, which is (Model|Data). Examples are GP, CNP.
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p585
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * The `Q' suffix means quality as log-complementary-phred-scale posterior probability, -10 log_10(Data|Model), where the model is the most likely genotype that appears in the GT field.
    Examples are GQ, CNQ.
    The fixed site-level QUAL field follows the same convention (represented as a phred-scaled number).
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p586
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] * The `L' prefix indicates the local-allele equivalent of a Number=A, R or G field.
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation::p587
text: [VCF v4.5 — Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation] § INFO KEYS USED FOR STRUCTURAL VARIANTS
section_id: Understanding the VCF format and the haplotype representation

text: [VCF v4.5 — VCF tag naming conventions] Several tag names follow conventions which should be used for implementation-defined tag as well:

[VCF v4.5 — VCF tag naming conventions] * The `L' suffix means likelihood as log-likelihood in the sampling distribution, log_10(Data|Model).
    Likelihoods are represented as log_10 scale, thus they are negative numbers (e.g. GL, CNL).
    The likelihood can be also represented in some cases as phred-scale in a separate tag (e.g. PL).
[VCF v4.5 — VCF tag naming conventions] * The `P' suffix means probability as linear-scale probability in the posterior distribution, which is (Model|Data). Examples are GP, CNP.

[VCF v4.5 — VCF tag naming conventions] * The `Q' suffix means quality as log-complementary-phred-scale posterior probability, -10 log_10(Data|Model), where the model is the most likely genotype that appears in the GT field.
    Examples are GQ, CNQ.
    The fixed site-level QUAL field follows the same convention (represented as a phred-scaled number).
[VCF v4.5 — VCF tag naming conventions] * The `L' prefix indicates the local-allele equivalent of a Number=A, R or G field.
[VCF v4.5 — VCF tag naming conventions] § INFO KEYS USED FOR STRUCTURAL VARIANTS

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p594
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] The following INFO keys are reserved for encoding structural variants.
In general, when these keys are used by imprecise variants, the values should be best estimates.
When present, per allele values must be specified for all ALT alleles (including non-structural alleles).
Except in lists of strings, the missing value should be used as a placeholder for the ALT alleles for which the key does not have a meaningful value.
The empty string should be used to encode missing values in lists of strings.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p595
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Indicates that this record contains an imprecise structural variant ALT allele. ALT alleles missing CIPOS are to be interpreted as imprecise variants with an unspecified confidence interval.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p596
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If a precise ALT allele is present in a record with the IMPRECISE flag, CIPOS must be explicitly set for that allele, even if it is `0,0`.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p597
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] END has been deprecated in favour of INFO SVLEN and FORMAT LEN.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p598
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] This field has been deprecated due to redundancy with ALT.
Refer to section <ref> for the set of valid ALT field symbolic structural variant alleles.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p599
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] One value for each ALT allele.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p600
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] SVLEN must be specified for symbolic structural variant alleles.
SVLEN is defined for INS, DUP, INV, and DEL symbolic alleles as the number of the inserted, duplicated, inverted, and deleted bases respectively.
SVLEN is defined for CNV symbolic alleles as the length of the segment over which the copy number variant is defined.
The missing value . should be used for all other ALT alleles, including ALT alleles using breakend notation.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p601
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] For backwards compatibility, a missing SVLEN should be inferred from the END field.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p602
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] For backwards compatibility, the absolute value of SVLEN should be taken and a negative SVLEN should be treated as positive values.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p603
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Note that for structural variant symbolic alleles, POS corresponds to the base immediately preceding the variant.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p604
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If present, the number of entries must be twice the number of ALT alleles.
CIPOS consists of successive pairs of records indicating the start and end offsets relative to POS of the confidence interval for each ALT allele.
For example, CIPOS=-5,5,0,0 indicates a 5bp confidence interval in each direction for the first ALT allele, and an exact position for the second alt allele.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p605
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] When breakpoint sequence homology exists, CIPOS should be used in conjunction with HOMSEQ to specify the interval of homology.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p606
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If both IMPRECISE and CIPOS are omitted, CIPOS is implicitly defined as 0,0 for all alleles.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p607
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Each CIPOS interval must span 0. That is, the lower bound cannot be greater than 0, and the upper bound cannot be less than 0.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p608
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If present, the number of entries must be twice the number of ALT alleles.
CIEND consists of successive pairs of records encoding the confidence interval start and end offsets relative to the END position inferred by SVLEN for each ALT allele.
For symbolic structural variants, the first in the pair must not be greater than 0, and the second must not be less than 0.
For all other alleles, both should be the missing value ..
For example, CIEND=-5,5,.,. indicates a 5bp confidence interval in each direction around the end position for the first ALT allele, and no CIEND is defined for the second alt allele.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p609
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If CIEND is missing, it is assumed to match CIPOS.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p610
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] For precise variants, the consensus sequence the alternate allele assembly is derivable from the REF and ALT fields.
However, the alternate allele assembly file may contain additional information about the characteristics of the alt allele contigs.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p611
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If present, the number of entries must be four (4) times the number of ALT alleles.
MEINFO consists of successive quadruplets of records for each ALT allele.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p612
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If present, the number of entries must be four (4) times the number of ALT alleles.
METRANS consists of successive quadruplets of records for each ALT allele.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p613
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Whilst simple events such as deletions and duplications can be wholly represented by a single VCF record, complex rearrangements such as chromothripsis result in a large number of breakpoints.
VCF uses the EVENT field to group such related records together, and EVENTTYPE to classify these events.
All records with the same EVENT value are considered to be part of the same event.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p614
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] The following EVENTTYPE values are reserved and should be used when appropriate:
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p615
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * DEL:ME - Deletion of mobile element with respect to the reference
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p616
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * INS:ME - Insertion of mobile element
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p617
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * DUP:TANDEM - Tandem duplication
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p618
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * DUP:DISPERSED - Dispersed duplication
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p619
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * TRA:BALANCED - Balanced inter-chromosomal translocation
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p620
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * TRA:UNBALANCED - Unbalanced inter-chromosomal translocation
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p621
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * BFB - breakage fusion bridge
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p622
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * DOUBLEMINUTE - Double minute
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p623
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] The semantics of other EVENTTYPE values is implementation-defined.
The use of EVENT is not restricted to structural variation and can also be used to associate non-symbolic alleles.
Such linking is useful for scenarios such as kataegis or when there is variant position ambiguity in segmentally duplicated regions.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p624
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If present, the number of entries must be twice the number of ALT alleles.
CILEN consists of successive pairs of records indicating the lower and upper bounds of the SVLEN confidence interval.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p625
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If present, the number of entries must be twice the number of ALT alleles.
CICN consists of successive pairs of records indicating the lower and upper copy number bounds.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p626
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] This field disambiguates the claim being made by DEL and DUP structural variants.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p627
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] The distinction between different claim types is made necessary by the fact that many structural variants affect both the overall abundance of a region and its adjacency structure, but often only one of those effects is being directly observed.
It is important to specify which one, because if this is not done, other variants in a genome may make the call ambiguous.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p628
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] For example, a “conventional” deletion usually both decreases the DNA abundance of a region and creates an adjacency between two sequence locations, which are the breakpoints of that deletion.
However, if a deletion or duplication is accompanied by a compensatory variant elsewhere in the genome, it may only cause changes in the adjacency structure, but not the overall copy number.
On the contrary, combinations of certain complex events in a genome may cause the overall copy number decrease, but not the linear excision of a sequence fragment.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p629
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] A copy number based duplication call does not make any claims on where the duplicated region is in the genome.
It could be a simple tandem duplication, a dispersed duplication, retrotransposition, or even form part of extra-chromosomal DNA such as a double minute.
In contrast, a breakpoint based duplication call requires that the end of the duplicated region be adjacent to the start of the duplicated region.
This rules out events such as dispersed duplication or retrotransposition, but is consistent with simple tandem duplications as well as double minutes.
Both deletion and duplication breakpoint calls can form part of more complex events that result in no copy number change in the deleted/duplicated region.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p630
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] To resolve any ambiguity in the interpretation of deletion and duplication symbolic alleles, three claim types are defined:
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p631
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * D (abundance / read Depth) claim indicates that the call has been made based only on a measure of DNA abundance of the called region, with no evidence to support changes in breakpoint structure. This includes indirect claims of abundance made using SNV variant allele frequency.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p632
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * J (adjacency / break Junction) claim indicates that the call has been made based on the detection of a non-reference DNA adjacency, with no evidence to support overall changes in DNA abundance.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p633
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * DJ indicates that there is evidence for both DNA abundance and adjacency changes, which are consistent with each other and suggest the structural variant of the type being reported.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p634
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Rules of applying SVCLAIM field are different depending on symbolic allele type:
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p635
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * DEL/DUP: SVCLAIM must be specified and can be be D, J, or DJ. J and DJ claims indicate a breakpoint between the start and end of the DEL/DUP.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p636
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * CNV: implicit abundance claim (SVCLAIM=D). SVCLAIM is optional but, if specified, must be D, or the missing value “.”, which is equivalent to D.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p637
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * INV/INS: implicit adjacency claims (SVCLAIM=J). SVCLAIM is optional but, if specified, must be J, DJ, or the missing value “.”, which is equivalent to J.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p638
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * breakend alleles are implicit adjacency claims (SVCLAIM=J). SVCLAIM is optional but, if specified, must be J, or the missing value “.”, which is equivalent to J.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p639
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] * Non-SV alleles should use the missing value “.”.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p640
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] If all alleles contain the missing value “.”, the SVCLAIM field can be omitted.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p641
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Note that the scope of SVCLAIM is the single ALT allele taken in isolation.
Complex events such as a cut and paste transposition of a mobile element result in multiple interrelated copy number and breakpoint claims.
These records should be linked using EVENT/EVENTTYPE, with the SVCLAIM field on each record specifying the claim of that individual allele record.
That is, a J claim must not be made on a CNV record even if the corresponding start/end breakpoints are known, and a DJ claim on a DEL or DUP is a claim of a simple, isolated deletion or tandem duplication.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p642
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Used by <CNV:TR> tandem repeat alleles to encode the number of repeat sequences.
This field determines the number of values encoded in the RUS, RUL, RB, CIRB, RUC and CIRUC fields for each allele.
The length of these fields must match the sum of all RN values for the record.
For the purposes of determining RUS, RUL, RB, CIRB, RUC and CIRUC lengths, the missing value “.” should be treated as 0.
If this field is missing, the RN for each <CNV:TR> allele is assumed to be 1 and 0 otherwise.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p643
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] See section <ref> for further details.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p644
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Used by <CNV:TR> tandem repeat alleles to encode information about the nature of the tandem repeats contained for ALT alleles.
Conceptually, these fields each contain a list of values for each ALT allele.
The length of these inner lists are determined by the RN field for that ALT allele and the length must match the sum of RN for the record.
These fields contain the flattened and concatenated list contents in the same order as either corresponding ALT allele.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p645
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Each <CNV:TR> allele consists of RN repeat sequences each containing RUC repeat units with sequence RUS.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p646
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] For example, if a <CNV:TR> allele sequence is (CAG)_10(TG)_7(CAGG)_3, the RN for that ALT allele would be 3, the RUS CAG,TG,CAGG, the RUL 3,2,4, the RUC 10,7,3 and the RB 30,14,12.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p647
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] RUS may contain only IUPAC nucleotide codes (ambiguous bases are allowed) or the missing value (`.').
If both RUS and RUL are present and not missing, the length of each RUS entry must match the corresponding RUL.
If both RUC and RB are present and not missing, RB should approximately equal RUL*RUC but is not required to match exactly.
If RUS is present but RUL is not, RUL should be inferred to be equal to the length of the corresponding RUS.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p648
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] See section <ref> for an example and further details.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p649
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] Confidence interval around RUC and RB respectively.
The length of these fields must be twice that of their corresponding fields.
CIRUC/CIRB must not be non-missing for any alleles with no corresponding RUC/RB value.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p650
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] These fields are defined in the same manner as CIPOS and contain the difference between the lower and upper confidence interval bounds and the value of the corresponding field.
The lower bound must be less than or equal to zero and the upper bound must be greater than or equal to zero.
If the lower bound is the missing value “.”, is assumed to be 0 and if the upper bound is the missing value “.”, it is assumed to be an unbounded estimate.
That is, the length of repeat has been determined to be at least a certain length but a reasonable limit of total length of the repeat could not be determined.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p651
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] See section <ref> for an example and further details.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p652
text:
[VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] This field encodes the length of each repeat unit for situations where the length of each repeat unit varies greatly.
If this field is present, RUC must also be present and must contain only integer values.
This field uses the same list-of-list encoding as RUS/RUL/RUC/RB but contains a list for each RC entry, the length of which is determined by the corresponding integer RUC value.
This field contains the length of each individual repeat unit for each RUC entry.
If RUB is missing or not specified, the RUB for each individual repeat unit is considered to be equal to the RUL for the corresponding repeat sequence.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p653
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] For the vast majority of tandem repeats, this field can be omitted and is only required in complex situations such as when a VNTR contains one or more variable length STRs.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p654
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] See section <ref> for an example and further details.
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::INFO keys used for structural variants::p655
text: [VCF v4.5 — INFO keys used for structural variants] § FORMAT KEYS USED FOR STRUCTURAL VARIANTS
section_id: INFO keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::FORMAT keys used for structural variants::p656
text:
[VCF v4.5 — FORMAT keys used for structural variants] CN specifies the total copy number over the region defined in the <CNV>, <DEL>, <DUP> alleles.
When FORMAT CN is present, all <CNV>, <DEL> and <DUP> alleles must have the same SVLEN.
CNQ, CNL, and CNP are analogous to GQ/GL/GP fields.
CNQ is encoded as a phred quality -10log_10 p(copy number genotype call is wrong).
CNL specifies a list of log_10 likelihoods for each potential copy number, starting from zero.
CNP is 0 to 1-scaled copy number posterior probabilities (and otherwise defined precisely as the CNL field), intended to store imputed genotype probabilities.
When possible, GT/GQ/GL/GP should be used instead of (or in addition to) these keys.
section_id: FORMAT keys used for structural variants

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::FORMAT keys used for structural variants::p657
text: [VCF v4.5 — FORMAT keys used for structural variants] § REPRESENTING VARIATION IN VCF RECORDS
section_id: FORMAT keys used for structural variants

text: [VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§ Creating VCF entries for SNPs and small indels
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] For example, suppose we are looking at a locus in the genome:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref     a t C g a     C is the reference base 
 1       a t G g a     C base is a G in some individuals 
 2       a t  -  g a     C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
 3       a t CAg a     A base is inserted w.r.t. the reference sequence
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Representing these as VCF records would be done as follows:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * A SNP polymorphism of C/G at position 3 where C is the reference base and G is the alternate base becomes REF=C, ALT=G
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * A single base deletion of C at position 3 becomes REF=TC, ALT=T
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * A single base insertion of A after position 3 becomes REF=C, ALT=CA
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Note that the positions must be sorted in increasing order:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     2     .     TC     T     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     3     .     C     G     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     3     .     C     CA     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Suppose I see a the following in a population of individuals and want to represent these three segregating alleles:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref     a t C g a     C is the reference base 
 1       a t G g a     C base is a G in some individuals 
 2       a t  -  g a     C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] In this case there are three segregating alleles: {tC,tG,t} with a corresponding VCF record:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     2     .     TC     TG,T     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Now suppose I have this more complex example:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref     a t C g a     C is the reference base 
 1       a t  -  g a     C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence 
 2       a t  - -  a     C and G bases are deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
 3       a t CAg a     A base is inserted w.r.t. the reference sequence

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] There are actually four segregating alleles: {tCg,tg,t,tCAg} over bases 2–4.
This complex set of allele is represented in VCF as:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     2     .     TCG     TG,T,TCAG     .     PASS     DP=100

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Note that in VCF records, the molecular equivalence explicitly listed above in the per-base alignment is discarded, so the actual placement of equivalent g isn't retained.
For completeness, VCF records are dynamically typed, so whether a VCF record is a SNP, Indel, Mixed, or Reference site depends on the properties of the alleles in the record.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§ Decoding VCF entries for SNPs and small indels
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Suppose I receive the following VCF record:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     3     .     C     T     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] This is a SNP since its only single base substitution and there are only two alleles so I have the two following segregating haplotypes:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref          C is the reference base 
 1          C base is a T in some individuals
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Suppose I receive the following VCF record:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     3     .     C     CTAG     .     PASS     DP=100

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] This is a insertion since the reference base C is being replaced by C [the reference base] plus three insertion bases TAG.
Again there are only two alleles so I have the two following segregating haplotypes:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref          C is the reference base 
 1          following the C base is an insertion of 3 bases
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Suppose I receive the following VCF record:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     2     .     TCG     T     .     PASS     DP=100

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] This is a deletion of two reference bases since the reference allele TCG is being replaced by just the T [the reference base].
Again there are only two alleles so I have the two following segregating haplotypes:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref          T is the (first) reference base 
 1          following the T base is a deletion of 2 bases
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§.§ Mixed VCF record for a microsatellite
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Suppose I receive the following VCF record:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 20     4     .     GCG     G,GCGCG     .     PASS     DP=100

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] This is a mixed type record containing a 2 base insertion and a 2 base deletion.
There are are three segregating alleles so I have the three following haplotypes:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref          G is the (first) reference base 
 1          following the G base is a deletion of 2 bases 
 2          following the G base is an insertion of 2 bases

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Note that in all of these examples dashes have been added to make the haplotypes clearer but of course the equivalence among bases isn't provided by the VCF.
Technically the following is an equivalent alignment:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref          G is the (first) reference base 
 1          following the G base is a deletion of 2 bases 
 2          following the G base is an insertion of 2 bases
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The following page contains examples of structural variants encoded in VCF, showing in order:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The same deletion in symbolic notation
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The same deletion split into constituent breakpoint and copy number records
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * A poly A expansion
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The same poly A expansion represented as a duplication
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * An insertion with both length and position imprecise
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The sequence of chrA in this example is ATGCGAAAAAAATGT.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§ Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] An arbitrary rearrangement can be summarized as a set of novel adjacencies. Each adjacency ties together 2 breakends.
The two breakends at either end of a novel adjacency are called mates.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] There is one line of VCF (i.e. one record) for each of the two breakends in a novel adjacency.
The REF field of a breakend record indicates a base or sequence s of bases beginning at position POS, as in all VCF records.
The ALT field of a breakend record indicates a replacement for s.
This “breakend replacement” has three parts:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The string t that replaces places s.
  The string t may be an extended version of s if some novel bases are inserted during the formation of the novel adjacency.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The position p of the mate breakend, indicated by a string of the form “chr:pos”.
  This is the location of the first mapped base in the piece being joined at this novel adjacency.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The direction that the joined sequence continues in, starting from p.
  This is indicated by the orientation of square brackets surrounding p.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] These 3 elements are combined in 4 possible ways to create the ALT.
In each of the 4 cases, the assertion is that s is replaced with t, and then some piece starting at position p is joined to t.
The cases are:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] s     t[p[     piece extending to the right of p is joined after t
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] s     t]p]     reverse comp piece extending left of p is joined after t
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] s     ]p]t     piece extending to the left of p is joined before t
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] s     [p[t     reverse comp piece extending right of p is joined before t

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The example in Figure 1 shows a 3-break operation involving 6 breakends.
It exemplifies all possible orientations of breakends in adjacencies.
Notice how the ALT field expresses the orientation of the breakends.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] All possible orientations of breakends
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM    POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]17:198982]     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     [17:198983[A     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 17     198982     bnd_Y     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 17     198983     bnd_Z     C     [13:123457[C     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Sometimes, as shown in Figure 2, some bases are inserted between the two breakends, this information is also carried in the ALT column:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]AGTNNNNNCAT     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     CAGTNNNNNCA[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] If the insertion is too long to be conveniently stored in the ALT column, as in the 329 base insertion shown in Figure 3, it can be represented by a contig from the assembly file:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:329]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Note: In the special case of the complete insertion of a sequence between two base pairs, it is recommended to use the shorthand notation described below:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     INS0     C     <ctg1>     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] If only a portion of <ctg1>, say from position 7 to position 214, is inserted, the VCF would be:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:7[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:214]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] If <ctg1> is circular and a segment from position 229 to position 45 is inserted, i.e., continuing from position 329 on to position 1, this is represented by adding a circular adjacency:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:229[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:45]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] <ctg1>     1     bnd_X     A     ]<ctg1>:329]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_Y;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] <ctg1>     329     bnd_Y     T     T[<ctg1>:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_C;EVENT=INS0

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] If a breakend has multiple mates such as in Figure 4 (either because of breakend reuse or of uncertainty in the measurement), these alternate adjacencies are treated as alternate alleles:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[,C[17:198983[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V,bnd_Z
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 17     198983     bnd_Z     A     ]13:123456]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Two breakends which are connected in the reference genome but disconnected in the variants are called partners.
Each breakend only has one partner, typically one basepair left or right.
However, it is not uncommon to observe loss of a few basepairs during the rearrangement.
A breakend's partner may be explicitly named as in Figure 5:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G[13:123460[     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_V;MATEID=bnd_X
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_W;MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_X;MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123460     bnd_X     A     ]2:321681]A     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_U;MATEID=bnd_W

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] For a rearrangement involving the telomere end of a reference chromosome, we define a virtual telomeric breakend that serves as a breakend partner for the breakend at the telomere.
That way every breakend has a partner.
If the chromosome extends from position 1 to N, then the virtual telomeric breakends are at positions 0 and N+1.
For example, to describe the reciprocal translocation of the entire chromosome 1 into chromosome 13, as illustrated in Figure 6:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] the records would look like:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     0     bnd_X     N     .[13:123457[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     1     bnd_Y     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[1:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_Y
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]1:0]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] As mentioned previously, a single rearrangement event can be described as a set of novel adjacencies.
For example, a reciprocal rearrangement such as in Figure 7:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G[13:123457[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     ]2:321681]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Similarly an inversion such as in Figure 8:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] can be described equivalently in two ways.
Either one uses the short hand notation described previously (recommended for simple cases):
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     INV0     G     <INV>     6     PASS     SVLEN=100000
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] or one describes the breakends:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§.§ Uncertainty around breakend location

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] It sometimes is difficult to determine the exact position of a break, generally because of homologies between the sequences being modified, such as in Figure 9.
The breakend is then placed arbitrarily at the left most position, and the uncertainty is represented with the CIPOS tag.
The ALT string is then constructed assuming this arbitrary breakend choice.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The figure above represents a nonreciprocal translocation with microhomology.
Even if we know that breakend U is rearranged with breakend V, actually placing these breaks can be extremely difficult.
The red and green dashed lines represent the most extreme possible recombination events which are allowed by the sequence evidence available.
We therefore place both U and V arbitrarily within the interval of possibility:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_V     T     T]13:123462]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;CIPOS=0,6
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     A     A]2:321687]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;CIPOS=0,6

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Note that the coordinate in breakend U's ALT string does not correspond to the designated position of breakend V, but to the position that V would take if U's position were fixed (and vice-versa).
The CIPOS tags describe the uncertainty around the positions of U and V.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The fact that breakends U and V are mates is preserved thanks to the MATEID tags.
If this were a reciprocal translocation, then there would be additional breakends X and Y, say with X the partner of V on Chr 2 and Y the partner of U on Chr 13, and there would be two more lines of VCF for the XY novel adjacency.
Depending on which positions are chosen for the breakends X and Y, it might not be obvious that X is the partner of V and Y is the partner of U from their locations alone.
This partner relationship can be specified explicitly with the tag PARID=bnd_X in the VCF line for breakend V and PARID=bnd_Y in the VCF line for breakend U, and vice versa.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] We allow for the definition of a breakend that is not part of a novel adjacency.
We call these single breakends, because they lack a mate.
Breakends that are unobserved partners of breakends in observed novel adjacencies are one kind of single breakend.
For example, if the true situation is known to be either as depicted back in Figure 1, and we only observe the adjacency (U,V), and no adjacencies for W, X, Y, or Z, then we cannot be sure whether we have a simple reciprocal translocation or a more complex 3-break operation.
Yet we know the partner X of U and the partner W of V exist and are breakends. In this case we can specify these as single breakends, with unknown mates.
The 4 lines of VCF representing this situation would be:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G.     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     .A     6     PASS     .

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] On the other hand, if we know a simple reciprocal translocation has occurred as in Figure 7, then even if we have no evidence for the (W,X) adjacency, for accounting purposes an adjacency between W and X may also be recorded in the VCF file.
These two breakends W and X can still be crossed-referenced as mates.
The 4 VCF records describing this situation would look exactly as in section <ref>, but perhaps with a special quality or filter value for the breakends W and X.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Another possible reason for calling single breakends is an observed but unexplained change in copy number along a chromosome.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 3     12665     bnd_X     A     .A     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 3     12665     .     A     <DUP>     14     PASS     SVCLAIM=D;SVLEN=1021;CIPOS=-50,50;CIEND=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 3     13686     bnd_Y     T     T.     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Finally, if an insertion is detected but only the first few base-pairs provided by overhanging reads could be assembled, then this inserted sequence can be provided on that line, in analogy to paired breakends:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 3     12665     bnd_X     A     .TGCA     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 3     12665     .     A     <DUP>     14     PASS     SVCLAIM=D;SVLEN=1021;CIPOS=-50,50;CIEND=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 3     13686     bnd_Y     T     TCC.     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] It may be extremely difficult to obtain clinically perfect samples, with only one type of cell.
Let's imagine that two samples are taken from a cancer patient: healthy blood, and some tumor tissue with an estimated 30% stromal contamination.
This would then be expressed in the header as:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Because of this distinction between sample and genome, it is possible to express the data along both distinctions.
For example, in a first pass, a structural variant caller would simply report counts per sample.
Using the example of the inversion just above, the VCF code could become:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Blood     TissueSample
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U     GT:AD     0:32,0     0|1:9,21
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X     GT:AD     0:29,0     0|1:11,25
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W     GT:AD     0:34,0     0|1:10,23
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V     GT:AD     0:31,0     0|1:8,20
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] However, a more evolved algorithm could attempt actually deconvolving the two genomes and generating copy number estimates based on the raw data:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Blood     TumorSample
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 13     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V     GT:CN     0:1     1:1

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] In cancer, each VCF file represents several genomes from a patient, but one genome is special in that it represents the germline genome of the patient.
This genome is contrasted to a second genome, the cancer tumor genome.
In the simplest case the VCF file for a single patient contains only these two genomes.
This is assumed in most of the discussion of the sections below.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] In general there may be several tumor genomes from the same patient in the VCF file.
Some of these may be secondary tumors derived from an original primary tumor.
We suggest the derivation relationships between genomes in a cancer VCF file be represented in the header with PEDIGREE tags.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Analogously, there might also be several normal genomes from the same patient in the VCF (typically double normal studies with blood and solid tissue samples).
These normal genomes are then considered to be derived from the original germline genome, which has to be inferred by parsimony.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The general format of a PEDIGREE line describing asexual, clonal derivation is:
