[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] This line asserts that the DNA in genome DerivedID is asexually or clonally derived with mutations from the DNA in genome OriginalID.
This is the asexual analog of the VCF format that has been proposed for family relationships between genomes, i.e., there is one entry per trio of the form:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Let's consider a cancer patient VCF file with 4 genomes: germline, primary_tumor, secondary_tumor1, and secondary_tumor2 as illustrated in Figure 10.
The primary_tumor is derived from the germline and the secondary tumors are each derived independently from the primary tumor, in all cases by clonal derivation with mutations.
The PEDIGREE lines would look like:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Alternately, if data on the genomes is compiled in a database, a simple pointer can be provided:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The most general form of a pedigree line is:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] This means that the genome SampleID is derived from the N ≥ 1 genomes Ancestor1, ..., AncestorN.
Based on these derivation relationships two new pieces of information can be specified.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Firstly, we wish to express the knowledge that a variant is novel to a genome, with respect to its parent genome.
Ideally, this could be derived by simply comparing the features on either genomes.
However, insufficient data or sample mixtures might prevent us from clearly determining at which stage a given variant appeared. This would be represented by a mutation quality score.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Secondly, we define a haplotype as a set of variants which are known to be on the same chromosome in the germline genome.
Haplotype identifiers must be unique across the germline genome, and are conserved along clonal lineages, regardless of mutations, rearrangements, or recombination.
In the case of the duplication of a region within a haplotype, one copy retains the original haplotype identifier, and the others are considered to be novel haplotypes with their own unique identifiers.
All these novel haplotypes have in common their haplotype ancestor in the parent genome.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§ Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] In order to report sequencing data evidence for both variant and non-variant positions in the genome, the VCF specification allows to represent blocks of reference-only calls in a single record using the <*> allele and the FORMAT LEN field.
The convention adopted here is to represent reference evidence as likelihoods against an unknown alternate allele represented as <*>.
Think of this as the likelihood for reference as compared to any other possible alternate allele (both SNP, indel, or otherwise).

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Positions implicitly called by a preceding <*> for a sample must have GT set to the missing value (`.') and have no FORMAT fields other than LAA present.
If LAA is present and a reference block start is being defined for a given sample, the <*> allele must be included as an LAA allele for that sample even though the GT is 0/0.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Reference blocks were originally introduced by the gVCF file format[<https://help.basespace.illumina.com/articles/descriptive/gvcf-files/>].
Unfortunately, gVCF has issues scaling to many samples as the use of INFO END to encode the reference block length requires the reference block length to be the same for all samples.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] To retain backwards compatibility with with gVCF,
the symbolic allele <NON_REF> should be treated as an alias of <*>
and a missing FORMAT LEN field should be inferred from the INFO END tag if present.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] An example with both FORMAT LEN and a redundant INFO END is given below:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Sample
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4370     .     G     <*>     .     .     END=4383     GT:DP:GQ:MIN_DP:PL:LEN     0/0:25:60:23:0,60,900;14
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4384     .     C     <*>     .     .     END=4388     GT:DP:GQ:MIN_DP:PL:LEN     0/0:25:45:25:0,42,630;4
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4389     .     T     TC,<*>     213.73     .     .     GT:DP:GQ:PL:LEN     0/1:23:99:51,0,36,93,92,86
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4390     .     C     <*>     .     .     END=4390     GT:DP:GQ:MIN_DP:PL:LEN     0/0:26:0:26:0,0,315;1
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4391     .     C     <*>     .     .     END=4395     GT:DP:GQ:MIN_DP:PL:LEN     0/0:27:63:27:0,63,945;4
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4396     .     G     C,<*>     0     .     .     GT:DP:GQ:MIN_DP:PL:LEN     0/0:24:52:0,52,95,66,95,97
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] 1     4397     .     T     <*>     .     .     END=4416     GT:DP:GQ:MIN_DP:PL:LEN     0/0:22:14:22:0,15,593;19

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] When base modification information is present in the FORMAT field of a reference block record, the base modification information apply to all applicable bases covered by that reference block.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] §.§ Representing copy number variation
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] To encode copy number variation, VCF uses <CNV>, <DEL> and <DUP> symbolic structural variant alleles, CN INFO and FORMAT fields.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Allele specific copy number is specified through a <CNV> ALT allele for each distinct allelic copy number.
INFO CN defines the allele specific copy number with FORMAT CN defining the overall copy number for that sample.
POS and INFO SVLEN specify the genomic interval over which the copy number is defined.
<DEL> and <DUP> copy number (SVCLAIM=D) alleles should be treated as <CNV> alleles that implicitly define INFO CN=0 and CN=2, respectively.
As with all symbolic structural variants, the starting position of the interval is the base immediately after POS.
For example, a region on chr1 from position 101 to 130 (both inclusive) with allele-specific copy numbers of 1 and 2 can be represented as follows:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] All <CNV> alleles in the same VCF record should have the same SVLEN.
To eliminate genotype ambiguity, copy number ALT alleles should not be mixed with other ALT alleles.
When only copy number ALT alleles are present in a VCF record, GT=0 is equivalent to a <CNV> ALT allele with INFO CN of 1 and should be treated identically.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] If only total copy number is known, the copy number of the segment should be defined with a single <CNV> ALT allele with a missing INFO CN field.
In the above example this corresponds to the following:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The granularity of copy number representation is explicitly not defined in these specifications.
Copy number segmentation can be base-pair accurate with even 1bp changes deletions resulting in new copy number segments, be at a highly granular megabase level of resolution, or anywhere in between.
When the bounds of a copy number segment is not known precisely, this should be encoded in the CIPOS and CILEN INFO fields.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] The repetitive nature of both Short Tandem Repeats (STRs) and Variable Number Tandem Repeats (VNTRs) makes identifying variants in these regions difficult.
As a result, specialised techniques have been developed to estimate the length and composition of these regions.
Many of these techniques result in imprecise variant calls which cannot be unambiguously represented with non-symbolic alleles.
To accommodate such variant calls, a the <CNV:TR> symbolic allele can be used.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] In general, tandem repeats can be represented in one or both of two complementary representations.
When the exact sequence is known, the variant can be represented as a non-symbolic ALT allele.
The variant can be represented either as a single VCF record containing the entire sequence in the ALT field, or over multiple phased records.
When the exact sequence is not known, or when reporting tandem repeat 'summary' information, the variant can be represented as <CNV:TR> copy number variants with the RN, RS, RL, RB, RC and RUL INFO fields encoding the nature of the repeat expansion/contraction.
The allele sequence of a <CNV:TR> record consists of the one or more repeat sequences, each of which consist of a single repeat unit repeated one or more times.
That is, <CNV:TR> records can encode multiple different repeat motifs in a single allele but do not support "nested" repeats.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Figure 11 outlines the field encoding for a STR locus with alleles of sequence CAGCAGCAGTTGTTG ((CAG)_4(TTG)_2), and CACACA ((CA)_3).
RN=2,1 indicates that the first allele has two repeat sequences and the second allele one.
RUS, RUL, and RUC fields encode the repeat unit sequence, length, and count within each repeat sequence.
RB encodes the length of each repeat sequence.
For loci whether the repeat length variable (such as VNTRs containing STRs), RUB encodes the length of each individual repeat unit which, in this example, is unnecessary since they all match their corresponding RUL.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] A tandem repeat allele can be described by both a <CNV:TR> 'summary' record as well as non-symbolic records.
When possible, these records should be phased with their corresponding <CNV:TR> record as outlined in the following example.
In the following example, the reference genome contains a (CAG)_10 repeat at positions 101 to 130 inclusive, the first allele expands this to (CAG)_30 and the second allele is missing a G from the 6th repeat unit (CAG)_5(CA)_1(CAG)_4:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * As with all symbolic structural variant alleles, the POS of the <CNV:TR> record is the base immediately preceding the tandem repeat

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The SVLEN of the <CNV:TR> is the length of the reference allele.
    It is not the length of the <CNV:TR> allele.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The SVLEN of the <CNV:TR> allele of a novel (with respect to the reference) tandem repeat should be 1.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The POS of the <CNV:TR> allele of a novel (with respect to the reference) tandem repeat should be the base immediately preceding the inserted tandem repeat sequence.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * Both a <CNV:TR> and one or more non-symbolic records encoding the tandem repeat can be present.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * <CNV:TR> and the non-symbolic records encoding the tandem repeat should be phased if possible.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * When both <CNV:TR> and the equivalent non-symbolic records are present, the <CNV:TR> should approximately encode the sequence but is not required to encode the sequence exactly.
	For example, SNVs and indels may be omitted in the <CNV:TR> record.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * Variant callers which do not report allele-specific tandem repeats should use a single <CNV:TR> ALT allele and the missing genotype for the GT field (for example, ./. if diploid).

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The INFO and FORMAT CN fields should be present for <CNV:TR> records (as they are <CNV> records) and, when present, must correspond to the sample allelic length divided by the reference allelic length.
	Note that CN FORMAT field represents the overall copy number and the INFO CN the allele-specific copy number.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * When benchmarking tandem repeats, the <CNV:TR> interval provides a region over which a set of (preferably phased) non-symbolic records can be compared against for length and sequence composition.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * RN encodes the number of records for each allele in the RUS, RUL, RUC, RB fields.
	Conceptually, this a mechanism to encode a list-of-list with flat list format used in VCF.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * STRs should use RUS, whereas VNTRs should use RUL to ensure the VCF records are not excessively large.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * RUL should be omitted when RUS is present (as it is redundant when RS is present).
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * RUS or RUL must be specified for each <CNV:TR>.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * Support for multiple levels of repeat nesting (such as STRs within VNTRs) is limited to the RUL repeat unit length field which allows the overall length of each top-level repeat unit to be encoded.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The POS and SVLEN of <CNV:TR> records should match the STR/VNTR reference catalog sizes for catalog-based callers.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * Variant normalisation has limited utility in regions of low complexity as almost identical haplotypes can have very different normalised representations.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] In some cases, it is desirable to report the full repeat sequence of all alleles at a given repeat locus in a single VCF records.
There are no restrictions on doing so and in the above example, instead of reporting precise_alt1 and precise_alt2, the variants can be represented directly in a single record with a REF of:

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] When the length or number of repeat units in a repeat sequence cannot be determined precisely, CIRB and/or CIRUC can be used to define the bounds.
For example, if the total number of CAG repeats at the above locus was at least 50 ((CAG)_50-) and the mostly likely number of repeats was 65, then the <CNV:TR> could be encoded as follows:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * RN was omitted as it is only required if at least one <CNV:TR> allele has RN greater than 1.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * The confidence interval bounds are relative to the nominal value.
[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] * A missing upper bound indicates the maximum length is not known.

[VCF v4.5 — Representing variation in VCF records] Exactly representing nested repeats results in the loss of some repeat information when representing with a <CNV:TR> record.
For repeats such as ((ACCGGC)_4(ACCAGT))_3-5, summarising the repeat structure in a <CNV:TR> record requires either unrolling the inner repeats, or treating each outer repeat as a separate repeat sequence (the full repeat structure can be stored in a caller-specific non-standard INFO field).
For many VNTRs, the critical information to retain is the length of each repeat unit.
This length information can be encoded in the RUB field.
For example, a 10,000bp VNTRs domain repeated 5 times, each repeat 500bp longer than the previous can be encoded as follows:

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Creating VCF entries for SNPs and small indels::p917
text: [VCF v4.5 — Creating VCF entries for SNPs and small indels] §.§ Decoding VCF entries for SNPs and small indels
section_id: Creating VCF entries for SNPs and small indels

text: [VCF v4.5 — Example 1] For example, suppose we are looking at a locus in the genome:

records.Alteration: C is the reference base
C base is a G in some individuals
C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
A base is inserted w.r.t. the reference sequence

text:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref     a t C g a     C is the reference base 
 1       a t G g a     C base is a G in some individuals 
 2       a t  -  g a     C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
 3       a t CAg a     A base is inserted w.r.t. the reference sequence
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] Representing these as VCF records would be done as follows:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] * A SNP polymorphism of C/G at position 3 where C is the reference base and G is the alternate base becomes REF=C, ALT=G
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] * A single base deletion of C at position 3 becomes REF=TC, ALT=T
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] * A single base insertion of A after position 3 becomes REF=C, ALT=CA
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] Note that the positions must be sorted in increasing order:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] 20     2     .     TC     T     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] 20     3     .     C     G     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Example 1] 20     3     .     C     CA     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Example 2] Suppose I see a the following in a population of individuals and want to represent these three segregating alleles:

records.Alteration: C is the reference base
C base is a G in some individuals
C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence

text:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 2] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref     a t C g a     C is the reference base 
 1       a t G g a     C base is a G in some individuals 
 2       a t  -  g a     C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
[VCF v4.5 — Example 2] In this case there are three segregating alleles: {tC,tG,t} with a corresponding VCF record:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 2] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Example 2] 20     2     .     TC     TG,T     .     PASS     DP=100
[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] Now suppose I have this more complex example:

records.Alteration: C is the reference base
C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
C and G bases are deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
A base is inserted w.r.t. the reference sequence

text:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] Example     Sequence     Alteration 
 Ref     a t C g a     C is the reference base 
 1       a t  -  g a     C base is deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence 
 2       a t  - -  a     C and G bases are deleted w.r.t. the reference sequence
 3       a t CAg a     A base is inserted w.r.t. the reference sequence

[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] There are actually four segregating alleles: {tCg,tg,t,tCAg} over bases 2–4.
This complex set of allele is represented in VCF as:
[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] 20     2     .     TCG     TG,T,TCAG     .     PASS     DP=100

[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] Note that in VCF records, the molecular equivalence explicitly listed above in the per-base alignment is discarded, so the actual placement of equivalent g isn't retained.
For completeness, VCF records are dynamically typed, so whether a VCF record is a SNP, Indel, Mixed, or Reference site depends on the properties of the alleles in the record.
[VCF v4.5 — Example 3] §.§ Decoding VCF entries for SNPs and small indels

text: [VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] The following page contains examples of structural variants encoded in VCF, showing in order:
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] * The same deletion in symbolic notation
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] * The same deletion split into constituent breakpoint and copy number records
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] * A poly A expansion
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] * The same poly A expansion represented as a duplication
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] * An insertion with both length and position imprecise
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] The sequence of chrA in this example is ATGCGAAAAAAATGT.
[VCF v4.5 — Encoding Structural Variants] §.§ Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] An arbitrary rearrangement can be summarized as a set of novel adjacencies. Each adjacency ties together 2 breakends.
The two breakends at either end of a novel adjacency are called mates.

records.Meaning: piece extending to the right of p is joined after t
reverse comp piece extending left of p is joined after t
piece extending to the left of p is joined before t
reverse comp piece extending right of p is joined before t

text:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] There is one line of VCF (i.e. one record) for each of the two breakends in a novel adjacency.
The REF field of a breakend record indicates a base or sequence s of bases beginning at position POS, as in all VCF records.
The ALT field of a breakend record indicates a replacement for s.
This “breakend replacement” has three parts:

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] * The string t that replaces places s.
  The string t may be an extended version of s if some novel bases are inserted during the formation of the novel adjacency.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] * The position p of the mate breakend, indicated by a string of the form “chr:pos”.
  This is the location of the first mapped base in the piece being joined at this novel adjacency.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] * The direction that the joined sequence continues in, starting from p.
  This is indicated by the orientation of square brackets surrounding p.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] These 3 elements are combined in 4 possible ways to create the ALT.
In each of the 4 cases, the assertion is that s is replaced with t, and then some piece starting at position p is joined to t.
The cases are:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] s     t[p[     piece extending to the right of p is joined after t
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] s     t]p]     reverse comp piece extending left of p is joined after t
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] s     ]p]t     piece extending to the left of p is joined before t
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] s     [p[t     reverse comp piece extending right of p is joined before t

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] The example in Figure 1 shows a 3-break operation involving 6 breakends.
It exemplifies all possible orientations of breakends in adjacencies.
Notice how the ALT field expresses the orientation of the breakends.
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] All possible orientations of breakends
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM    POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]17:198982]     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     [17:198983[A     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 17     198982     bnd_Y     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 17     198983     bnd_Z     C     [13:123457[C     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Sometimes, as shown in Figure 2, some bases are inserted between the two breakends, this information is also carried in the ALT column:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]AGTNNNNNCAT     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     CAGTNNNNNCA[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] If the insertion is too long to be conveniently stored in the ALT column, as in the 329 base insertion shown in Figure 3, it can be represented by a contig from the assembly file:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:329]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Note: In the special case of the complete insertion of a sequence between two base pairs, it is recommended to use the shorthand notation described below:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     INS0     C     <ctg1>     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] If only a portion of <ctg1>, say from position 7 to position 214, is inserted, the VCF would be:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:7[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:214]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] If <ctg1> is circular and a segment from position 229 to position 45 is inserted, i.e., continuing from position 329 on to position 1, this is represented by adding a circular adjacency:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:229[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:45]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] <ctg1>     1     bnd_X     A     ]<ctg1>:329]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_Y;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] <ctg1>     329     bnd_Y     T     T[<ctg1>:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_C;EVENT=INS0

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] If a breakend has multiple mates such as in Figure 4 (either because of breakend reuse or of uncertainty in the measurement), these alternate adjacencies are treated as alternate alleles:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[,C[17:198983[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V,bnd_Z
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 17     198983     bnd_Z     A     ]13:123456]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Two breakends which are connected in the reference genome but disconnected in the variants are called partners.
Each breakend only has one partner, typically one basepair left or right.
However, it is not uncommon to observe loss of a few basepairs during the rearrangement.
A breakend's partner may be explicitly named as in Figure 5:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G[13:123460[     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_V;MATEID=bnd_X
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_W;MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_X;MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123460     bnd_X     A     ]2:321681]A     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_U;MATEID=bnd_W

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] For a rearrangement involving the telomere end of a reference chromosome, we define a virtual telomeric breakend that serves as a breakend partner for the breakend at the telomere.
That way every breakend has a partner.
If the chromosome extends from position 1 to N, then the virtual telomeric breakends are at positions 0 and N+1.
For example, to describe the reciprocal translocation of the entire chromosome 1 into chromosome 13, as illustrated in Figure 6:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] the records would look like:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 1     0     bnd_X     N     .[13:123457[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 1     1     bnd_Y     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[1:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_Y
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]1:0]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] As mentioned previously, a single rearrangement event can be described as a set of novel adjacencies.
For example, a reciprocal rearrangement such as in Figure 7:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G[13:123457[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     ]2:321681]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Similarly an inversion such as in Figure 8:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] can be described equivalently in two ways.
Either one uses the short hand notation described previously (recommended for simple cases):
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     INV0     G     <INV>     6     PASS     SVLEN=100000
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] or one describes the breakends:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] §.§.§ Uncertainty around breakend location

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] It sometimes is difficult to determine the exact position of a break, generally because of homologies between the sequences being modified, such as in Figure 9.
The breakend is then placed arbitrarily at the left most position, and the uncertainty is represented with the CIPOS tag.
The ALT string is then constructed assuming this arbitrary breakend choice.
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] The figure above represents a nonreciprocal translocation with microhomology.
Even if we know that breakend U is rearranged with breakend V, actually placing these breaks can be extremely difficult.
The red and green dashed lines represent the most extreme possible recombination events which are allowed by the sequence evidence available.
We therefore place both U and V arbitrarily within the interval of possibility:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_V     T     T]13:123462]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;CIPOS=0,6
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     A     A]2:321687]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;CIPOS=0,6

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Note that the coordinate in breakend U's ALT string does not correspond to the designated position of breakend V, but to the position that V would take if U's position were fixed (and vice-versa).
The CIPOS tags describe the uncertainty around the positions of U and V.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] The fact that breakends U and V are mates is preserved thanks to the MATEID tags.
If this were a reciprocal translocation, then there would be additional breakends X and Y, say with X the partner of V on Chr 2 and Y the partner of U on Chr 13, and there would be two more lines of VCF for the XY novel adjacency.
Depending on which positions are chosen for the breakends X and Y, it might not be obvious that X is the partner of V and Y is the partner of U from their locations alone.
This partner relationship can be specified explicitly with the tag PARID=bnd_X in the VCF line for breakend V and PARID=bnd_Y in the VCF line for breakend U, and vice versa.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] We allow for the definition of a breakend that is not part of a novel adjacency.
We call these single breakends, because they lack a mate.
Breakends that are unobserved partners of breakends in observed novel adjacencies are one kind of single breakend.
For example, if the true situation is known to be either as depicted back in Figure 1, and we only observe the adjacency (U,V), and no adjacencies for W, X, Y, or Z, then we cannot be sure whether we have a simple reciprocal translocation or a more complex 3-break operation.
Yet we know the partner X of U and the partner W of V exist and are breakends. In this case we can specify these as single breakends, with unknown mates.
The 4 lines of VCF representing this situation would be:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G.     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     .A     6     PASS     .

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] On the other hand, if we know a simple reciprocal translocation has occurred as in Figure 7, then even if we have no evidence for the (W,X) adjacency, for accounting purposes an adjacency between W and X may also be recorded in the VCF file.
These two breakends W and X can still be crossed-referenced as mates.
The 4 VCF records describing this situation would look exactly as in section <ref>, but perhaps with a special quality or filter value for the breakends W and X.
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Another possible reason for calling single breakends is an observed but unexplained change in copy number along a chromosome.
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 3     12665     bnd_X     A     .A     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 3     12665     .     A     <DUP>     14     PASS     SVCLAIM=D;SVLEN=1021;CIPOS=-50,50;CIEND=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 3     13686     bnd_Y     T     T.     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Finally, if an insertion is detected but only the first few base-pairs provided by overhanging reads could be assembled, then this inserted sequence can be provided on that line, in analogy to paired breakends:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 3     12665     bnd_X     A     .TGCA     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 3     12665     .     A     <DUP>     14     PASS     SVCLAIM=D;SVLEN=1021;CIPOS=-50,50;CIEND=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 3     13686     bnd_Y     T     TCC.     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] It may be extremely difficult to obtain clinically perfect samples, with only one type of cell.
Let's imagine that two samples are taken from a cancer patient: healthy blood, and some tumor tissue with an estimated 30% stromal contamination.
This would then be expressed in the header as:

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Because of this distinction between sample and genome, it is possible to express the data along both distinctions.
For example, in a first pass, a structural variant caller would simply report counts per sample.
Using the example of the inversion just above, the VCF code could become:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Blood     TissueSample
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U     GT:AD     0:32,0     0|1:9,21
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X     GT:AD     0:29,0     0|1:11,25
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W     GT:AD     0:34,0     0|1:10,23
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V     GT:AD     0:31,0     0|1:8,20

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] However, a more evolved algorithm could attempt actually deconvolving the two genomes and generating copy number estimates based on the raw data:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Blood     TumorSample
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] 13     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V     GT:CN     0:1     1:1

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] In cancer, each VCF file represents several genomes from a patient, but one genome is special in that it represents the germline genome of the patient.
This genome is contrasted to a second genome, the cancer tumor genome.
In the simplest case the VCF file for a single patient contains only these two genomes.
This is assumed in most of the discussion of the sections below.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] In general there may be several tumor genomes from the same patient in the VCF file.
Some of these may be secondary tumors derived from an original primary tumor.
We suggest the derivation relationships between genomes in a cancer VCF file be represented in the header with PEDIGREE tags.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Analogously, there might also be several normal genomes from the same patient in the VCF (typically double normal studies with blood and solid tissue samples).
These normal genomes are then considered to be derived from the original germline genome, which has to be inferred by parsimony.
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] The general format of a PEDIGREE line describing asexual, clonal derivation is:

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] This line asserts that the DNA in genome DerivedID is asexually or clonally derived with mutations from the DNA in genome OriginalID.
This is the asexual analog of the VCF format that has been proposed for family relationships between genomes, i.e., there is one entry per trio of the form:

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Let's consider a cancer patient VCF file with 4 genomes: germline, primary_tumor, secondary_tumor1, and secondary_tumor2 as illustrated in Figure 10.
The primary_tumor is derived from the germline and the secondary tumors are each derived independently from the primary tumor, in all cases by clonal derivation with mutations.
The PEDIGREE lines would look like:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Alternately, if data on the genomes is compiled in a database, a simple pointer can be provided:
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] The most general form of a pedigree line is:

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] This means that the genome SampleID is derived from the N ≥ 1 genomes Ancestor1, ..., AncestorN.
Based on these derivation relationships two new pieces of information can be specified.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Firstly, we wish to express the knowledge that a variant is novel to a genome, with respect to its parent genome.
Ideally, this could be derived by simply comparing the features on either genomes.
However, insufficient data or sample mixtures might prevent us from clearly determining at which stage a given variant appeared. This would be represented by a mutation quality score.

[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] Secondly, we define a haplotype as a set of variants which are known to be on the same chromosome in the germline genome.
Haplotype identifiers must be unique across the germline genome, and are conserved along clonal lineages, regardless of mutations, rearrangements, or recombination.
In the case of the duplication of a region within a haplotype, one copy retains the original haplotype identifier, and the others are considered to be novel haplotypes with their own unique identifiers.
All these novel haplotypes have in common their haplotype ancestor in the parent genome.
[VCF v4.5 — Specifying complex rearrangements with breakends] §.§ Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)
[VCF v4.5 — Inserted Sequence] Sometimes, as shown in Figure 2, some bases are inserted between the two breakends, this information is also carried in the ALT column:
[VCF v4.5 — Inserted Sequence] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Inserted Sequence] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Inserted Sequence] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]AGTNNNNNCAT     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Inserted Sequence] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     CAGTNNNNNCA[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V

[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] If the insertion is too long to be conveniently stored in the ALT column, as in the 329 base insertion shown in Figure 3, it can be represented by a contig from the assembly file:
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:329]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] Note: In the special case of the complete insertion of a sequence between two base pairs, it is recommended to use the shorthand notation described below:
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123456     INS0     C     <ctg1>     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] If only a portion of <ctg1>, say from position 7 to position 214, is inserted, the VCF would be:
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:7[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:214]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V

[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] If <ctg1> is circular and a segment from position 229 to position 45 is inserted, i.e., continuing from position 329 on to position 1, this is represented by adding a circular adjacency:
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[<ctg1>:229[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]<ctg1>:45]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] <ctg1>     1     bnd_X     A     ]<ctg1>:329]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_Y;EVENT=INS0
[VCF v4.5 — Large Insertions] <ctg1>     329     bnd_Y     T     T[<ctg1>:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_C;EVENT=INS0

[VCF v4.5 — Multiple mates] If a breakend has multiple mates such as in Figure 4 (either because of breakend reuse or of uncertainty in the measurement), these alternate adjacencies are treated as alternate alleles:
[VCF v4.5 — Multiple mates] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Multiple mates] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Multiple mates] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Multiple mates] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[,C[17:198983[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V,bnd_Z
[VCF v4.5 — Multiple mates] 17     198983     bnd_Z     A     ]13:123456]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U

[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] Two breakends which are connected in the reference genome but disconnected in the variants are called partners.
Each breakend only has one partner, typically one basepair left or right.
However, it is not uncommon to observe loss of a few basepairs during the rearrangement.
A breakend's partner may be explicitly named as in Figure 5:
[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G[13:123460[     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_V;MATEID=bnd_X
[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_W;MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_X;MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Explicit partners] 13     123460     bnd_X     A     ]2:321681]A     6     PASS     PARID=bnd_U;MATEID=bnd_W

[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] For a rearrangement involving the telomere end of a reference chromosome, we define a virtual telomeric breakend that serves as a breakend partner for the breakend at the telomere.
That way every breakend has a partner.
If the chromosome extends from position 1 to N, then the virtual telomeric breakends are at positions 0 and N+1.
For example, to describe the reciprocal translocation of the entire chromosome 1 into chromosome 13, as illustrated in Figure 6:
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] the records would look like:
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] 1     0     bnd_X     N     .[13:123457[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] 1     1     bnd_Y     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[1:1[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_Y
[VCF v4.5 — Telomeres] 13     123457     bnd_V     A     ]1:0]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X

[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] As mentioned previously, a single rearrangement event can be described as a set of novel adjacencies.
For example, a reciprocal rearrangement such as in Figure 7:
[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G[13:123457[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Event modifiers] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     ]2:321681]A     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W;EVENT=RR0
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] Similarly an inversion such as in Figure 8:
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] can be described equivalently in two ways.
Either one uses the short hand notation described previously (recommended for simple cases):
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] 2     321681     INV0     G     <INV>     6     PASS     SVLEN=100000
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] or one describes the breakends:
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] 2     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] 2     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;EVENT=INV0
[VCF v4.5 — Inversions] §.§.§ Uncertainty around breakend location

[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] It sometimes is difficult to determine the exact position of a break, generally because of homologies between the sequences being modified, such as in Figure 9.
The breakend is then placed arbitrarily at the left most position, and the uncertainty is represented with the CIPOS tag.
The ALT string is then constructed assuming this arbitrary breakend choice.
[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] < g r a p h i c s >

[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] The figure above represents a nonreciprocal translocation with microhomology.
Even if we know that breakend U is rearranged with breakend V, actually placing these breaks can be extremely difficult.
The red and green dashed lines represent the most extreme possible recombination events which are allowed by the sequence evidence available.
We therefore place both U and V arbitrarily within the interval of possibility:
[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] 2     321681     bnd_V     T     T]13:123462]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U;CIPOS=0,6
[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] 13     123456     bnd_U     A     A]2:321687]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V;CIPOS=0,6

[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] Note that the coordinate in breakend U's ALT string does not correspond to the designated position of breakend V, but to the position that V would take if U's position were fixed (and vice-versa).
The CIPOS tags describe the uncertainty around the positions of U and V.

[VCF v4.5 — Uncertainty around breakend location] The fact that breakends U and V are mates is preserved thanks to the MATEID tags.
If this were a reciprocal translocation, then there would be additional breakends X and Y, say with X the partner of V on Chr 2 and Y the partner of U on Chr 13, and there would be two more lines of VCF for the XY novel adjacency.
Depending on which positions are chosen for the breakends X and Y, it might not be obvious that X is the partner of V and Y is the partner of U from their locations alone.
This partner relationship can be specified explicitly with the tag PARID=bnd_X in the VCF line for breakend V and PARID=bnd_Y in the VCF line for breakend U, and vice versa.

[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] We allow for the definition of a breakend that is not part of a novel adjacency.
We call these single breakends, because they lack a mate.
Breakends that are unobserved partners of breakends in observed novel adjacencies are one kind of single breakend.
For example, if the true situation is known to be either as depicted back in Figure 1, and we only observe the adjacency (U,V), and no adjacencies for W, X, Y, or Z, then we cannot be sure whether we have a simple reciprocal translocation or a more complex 3-break operation.
Yet we know the partner X of U and the partner W of V exist and are breakends. In this case we can specify these as single breakends, with unknown mates.
The 4 lines of VCF representing this situation would be:
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G.     6     PASS     .
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     ]13:123456]T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 13     123456     bnd_U     C     C[2:321682[     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 13     123457     bnd_X     A     .A     6     PASS     .

[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] On the other hand, if we know a simple reciprocal translocation has occurred as in Figure 7, then even if we have no evidence for the (W,X) adjacency, for accounting purposes an adjacency between W and X may also be recorded in the VCF file.
These two breakends W and X can still be crossed-referenced as mates.
The 4 VCF records describing this situation would look exactly as in section <ref>, but perhaps with a special quality or filter value for the breakends W and X.
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] Another possible reason for calling single breakends is an observed but unexplained change in copy number along a chromosome.
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 3     12665     bnd_X     A     .A     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 3     12665     .     A     <DUP>     14     PASS     SVCLAIM=D;SVLEN=1021;CIPOS=-50,50;CIEND=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 3     13686     bnd_Y     T     T.     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50

[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] Finally, if an insertion is detected but only the first few base-pairs provided by overhanging reads could be assembled, then this inserted sequence can be provided on that line, in analogy to paired breakends:
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 3     12665     bnd_X     A     .TGCA     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 3     12665     .     A     <DUP>     14     PASS     SVCLAIM=D;SVLEN=1021;CIPOS=-50,50;CIEND=-50,50
[VCF v4.5 — Single breakends] 3     13686     bnd_Y     T     TCC.     6     PASS     CIPOS=-50,50

[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] It may be extremely difficult to obtain clinically perfect samples, with only one type of cell.
Let's imagine that two samples are taken from a cancer patient: healthy blood, and some tumor tissue with an estimated 30% stromal contamination.
This would then be expressed in the header as:

[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] Because of this distinction between sample and genome, it is possible to express the data along both distinctions.
For example, in a first pass, a structural variant caller would simply report counts per sample.
Using the example of the inversion just above, the VCF code could become:
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Blood     TissueSample
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U     GT:AD     0:32,0     0|1:9,21
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X     GT:AD     0:29,0     0|1:11,25
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 13     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W     GT:AD     0:34,0     0|1:10,23
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 13     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V     GT:AD     0:31,0     0|1:8,20
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] However, a more evolved algorithm could attempt actually deconvolving the two genomes and generating copy number estimates based on the raw data:
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] #CHROM     POS     ID     REF     ALT     QUAL     FILTER     INFO     FORMAT     Blood     TumorSample
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 2     321681     bnd_W     G     G]2:421681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_U     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 2     321682     bnd_V     T     [2:421682[T     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_X     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 13     421681     bnd_U     A     A]2:321681]     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_W     GT:CN     0:1     1:1
[VCF v4.5 — Sample mixtures] 13     421682     bnd_X     C     [2:321682[C     6     PASS     MATEID=bnd_V     GT:CN     0:1     1:1

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] In cancer, each VCF file represents several genomes from a patient, but one genome is special in that it represents the germline genome of the patient.
This genome is contrasted to a second genome, the cancer tumor genome.
In the simplest case the VCF file for a single patient contains only these two genomes.
This is assumed in most of the discussion of the sections below.

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] In general there may be several tumor genomes from the same patient in the VCF file.
Some of these may be secondary tumors derived from an original primary tumor.
We suggest the derivation relationships between genomes in a cancer VCF file be represented in the header with PEDIGREE tags.

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] Analogously, there might also be several normal genomes from the same patient in the VCF (typically double normal studies with blood and solid tissue samples).
These normal genomes are then considered to be derived from the original germline genome, which has to be inferred by parsimony.
[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] The general format of a PEDIGREE line describing asexual, clonal derivation is:

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] This line asserts that the DNA in genome DerivedID is asexually or clonally derived with mutations from the DNA in genome OriginalID.
This is the asexual analog of the VCF format that has been proposed for family relationships between genomes, i.e., there is one entry per trio of the form:

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] Let's consider a cancer patient VCF file with 4 genomes: germline, primary_tumor, secondary_tumor1, and secondary_tumor2 as illustrated in Figure 10.
The primary_tumor is derived from the germline and the secondary tumors are each derived independently from the primary tumor, in all cases by clonal derivation with mutations.
The PEDIGREE lines would look like:
[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] < g r a p h i c s >
[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] Alternately, if data on the genomes is compiled in a database, a simple pointer can be provided:
[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] The most general form of a pedigree line is:

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] This means that the genome SampleID is derived from the N ≥ 1 genomes Ancestor1, ..., AncestorN.
Based on these derivation relationships two new pieces of information can be specified.

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] Firstly, we wish to express the knowledge that a variant is novel to a genome, with respect to its parent genome.
Ideally, this could be derived by simply comparing the features on either genomes.
However, insufficient data or sample mixtures might prevent us from clearly determining at which stage a given variant appeared. This would be represented by a mutation quality score.

[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] Secondly, we define a haplotype as a set of variants which are known to be on the same chromosome in the germline genome.
Haplotype identifiers must be unique across the germline genome, and are conserved along clonal lineages, regardless of mutations, rearrangements, or recombination.
In the case of the duplication of a region within a haplotype, one copy retains the original haplotype identifier, and the others are considered to be novel haplotypes with their own unique identifiers.
All these novel haplotypes have in common their haplotype ancestor in the parent genome.
[VCF v4.5 — Clonal derivation relationships] §.§ Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)::p1243
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)] In order to report sequencing data evidence for both variant and non-variant positions in the genome, the VCF specification allows to represent blocks of reference-only calls in a single record using the <*> allele and the FORMAT LEN field.
The convention adopted here is to represent reference evidence as likelihoods against an unknown alternate allele represented as <*>.
Think of this as the likelihood for reference as compared to any other possible alternate allele (both SNP, indel, or otherwise).
section_id: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

tables.section: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)::p1244
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)] Positions implicitly called by a preceding <*> for a sample must have GT set to the missing value (`.') and have no FORMAT fields other than LAA present.
If LAA is present and a reference block start is being defined for a given sample, the <*> allele must be included as an LAA allele for that sample even though the GT is 0/0.
section_id: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)::p1245
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)] Reference blocks were originally introduced by the gVCF file format[<https://help.basespace.illumina.com/articles/descriptive/gvcf-files/>].
Unfortunately, gVCF has issues scaling to many samples as the use of INFO END to encode the reference block length requires the reference block length to be the same for all samples.
section_id: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)::p1246
text:
[VCF v4.5 — Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)] To retain backwards compatibility with with gVCF,
the symbolic allele <NON_REF> should be treated as an alias of <*>
and a missing FORMAT LEN field should be inferred from the INFO END tag if present.
section_id: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)::p1247
text: [VCF v4.5 — Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)] An example with both FORMAT LEN and a redundant INFO END is given below:
section_id: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)

chunk_id: VCFv4.5.tex::Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)::p1248

section_id: Representing unspecified alleles and REF-only blocks (gVCF)
